The Art and Architecture of the Amarna Period

The earliest representations of Amenhotep IV show him in a traditional style similar to that used for Thutmose III and Amenhotep III. However, not long after his accession, Amenhotep IV was depicted with a distinctive appearance, characterized by a thin face, pointed chin, thick lips, an elongated neck, broad hips, a prominent belly, and slender legs. At first, the new style was still fairly restrained, but on most of the Theban monuments and during the early years at Amarna, the king's features were depicted in such an exaggerated way that made him appear as a caricature. Later in his reign, a more balanced style developed. A characteristic feature of the Amarna style is its extraordinary sense of movement and speed, a general 'looseness' and freedom of expression that was to have a lasting influence on Egyptian art for centuries after the Amarna period had come to an end.

The earliest structures of Amenhotep IV employed traditional large sandstone blocks commonly used for temple walls. However, these were soon replaced in both Thebes and Amarna by much smaller blocks, known as talatat, which typically measured around 60 x 25 cm. He was small enough for a single man to lift and carry. The new method was abandoned after the Amar Na Period, likely because it had become apparent that the reliefs carved on walls constructed with small blocks required extensive plaster finishing to fill the gaps between individual stones. These reliefs did not hold up as well as traditionally built walls. The temples and palaces are a different matter.

Akhenaten's god was visible to all, making a man-made cult image unnecessary. Every day, he would drive his chariot along the royal road from the North Riverside Palace to the Central City. This replaced the traditional divine processions, where townspeople could meet the gods whose statues were usually kept hidden in the temple. It is notable that these offering formulas were frequently addressed to the god by the king himself, rather than by the tomb owner.

The Aten was a god of life-giving light; during the night, he was absent, but it is unclear where he was thought to go. The mode of existence of the dead was one of a continual presence with the Aten and the king in the temple, where their ba-souls fed on daily offerings. The temples and palaces of Akhetaten were considered the new hereafter; the dead no longer lived in their tombs but among the living.

The tombs served only as their nightly resting places. Mummification persisted because at night the ba returned to the body until sunrise. For this reason, funerary rites, including offerings and tomb equipment, also appeared, although most shabti figures no longer had the chapter from the Book of the Dead inscribed on them.

There was no "judgement of the dead" before the throne of Osiris, which the deceased formerly had to pass through in order to gain the status of a maaty (‘righteous one’); instead, the king's officials earned their life after death by following Akhenaten's teachings and being totally loyal to him during their lifetime. Akhenaten was the god who granted life and a burial after old age in his favor. He embodied maat, and it was through loyalty to him that his subjects could attain maat. Life outside Amarna during the Amarna Period is largely unknown. Most of our knowledge of Akhenaten's new religion comes from his early monuments at Thebes and from the city itself, including its own ruins.

The events that occurred in the rest of the country, particularly after the king's relocation to his new city, are not well documented. Some Memphite blocks display the late form of the Aten name (after regnal year 9), and a stray block from Thebes also has this form. Therefore, even after the radicalization of Akhenaten's reform, construction work outside Amarna is obvious. It is not always easy to determine whether tomb reliefs, stelae, and artifacts of burial equipment that mention the Aten together with traditional gods such as Osiris, Thoth, or Ptah date from the beginning of a pharaoh's reign or later, or even from the time immediately following the Amarna Period.

It is also unclear what happened to the civil administration during the Amarna period. The situation during the Saite period may afford a parallel: the 26th-dynasty kings greatly favored Sais, their hometown (although they were originally of Libyan descent), which functioned as their capital, and much of the state revenues went to the temple of its goddess Neith. The aftermath of the Amarna period, although lasting barely twenty years, had a significant impact.

Plan of a group of tombs in the New Kingdom necropolis at Saqqara, where many important officials of the late 18th and 19th-20th Dynasties were buried. The reliefs and paintings on the walls of the tombs were no longer primarily concerned with scenes from the owner's career and professional occupation, although such scenes do not disappear completely. Instead, they concentrate on showing him adoring Ra, Osiris, and a wide variety of other gods and goddesses, wearing a long pleated linen costume (often mistakenly referred to as the 'dress of daily life') and an elaborate wig. These features may be seen as a reaction against Akhenaten's total monopoly over the funerary cult of his subjects, and the role that the Aten temples played in Amarna religion as the new 'hereafter'. Before we can discuss this development, however, we must return to the political and dynastic history of Egypt following the end of the Amarna period.

Tutankhamun, the young Tutankhaten, still a child, had ascended the throne at Amarna, but soon afterwards, perhaps as early as his first regnal year or shortly thereafter, he abandoned the city founded by his father. Both Thutmose III and Amenhotep III were very young when they ascended to the throne. In both cases, a senior female member of the royal family played a regent role during their early years. Hatshepsut and Mutemwiya served in this capacity for Thutmose III and Amenhotep III, respectively.

Horemheb would eventually become king himself. In his Coronation Text, a unique inscription detailing his rise to power, carved on the back of a statue now in the Egyptian Museum, Turin, he suggests that it was he who advised the king to abandon Amarna after chaos broke out in the palace following the deaths of Akhenaten and his successor. The gods had, therefore, abandoned Egypt. If one prayed to them, they no longer answered. When the army was sent to Syria to expand Egypt's borders, it met with no success. During Akhenaten's reign, Egypt's ally Mitanni was defeated by the Hittites, making them the dominant power in the north.

Some measures later described in Horemheb's Coronation Text and in his great Karnak Edict may have been carried out during the reign of Tutankhamun. Instead, Ay, a senior court adviser who had been one of Akhenaten's most trusted officials and may have been a relative of Amenhotep III's wife, Queen Tiy, conducted the obsequies and shortly afterwards ascended the throne. He also made a conscious effort to prevent Horemheb from asserting his rights after his death, for he appointed an army commander called Nakhtmin, who was possibly his grandson. Horemheb succeeded in ascending the throne after Ay's demise. He then set out to deface the monuments of his predecessor, Ay, and destroy those of his rival, Nekhbet (not Nakhtmin).

The unfinished state of his royal tomb in the Valley of the Kings (KV 57) is difficult to reconcile with such a long reign. Trouble with the Hittites over territories in northern Syria. Around regnal year 10, the Egyptians made an unsuccessful attempt to reconquer Qadesh and Amurru. It is typical of the reign that our sources for this confrontation are Hittite, rather than Egyptian texts.

It is even possible that Horemheb finally came to an agreement with his enemy, for a later Hittite text refers to a treaty that had been in force before it was broken during the reigns of Muwatalli and Sety I (1294-1279 BC). The Great Edict, which he published on a stele in the temple of Karnak, lists numerous legal measures aimed at curbing abuses such as the unlawful seizure of boats and slaves, cattle hide theft, illegal taxation of private farmland, and fraudulent assessment of lawful taxes. Additionally, it addresses extortion by officials organizing the king's annual visit to the Opet Festival during his journeys from Memphis to Thebes and back. The most significant feature of Horemheb's reign was his ability to legitimize it. As a non-royal individual, he could not claim a 'genealogical' link with the dynastic god Amun.

Horus of Hatusi presents him to Amun during the Opet Festival procession, and then crowns him as king. Horemheb owed his kingship to the will of his personal god and to divine election, as indicated by an oracle during a public appearance of Amun. Horemheb's coronation bears resemblance to that of Hatshepsut, who was elected by an oracle of Amun after serving as regent. Hatshepsut was at least able to claim to be of royal blood herself. She emphasized that Amun had fathered her as the daughter of the queen mother. This is a topic that Horemheb deliberately avoids discussing in his coronation text.

Rameses I: The principle of electing a non-royal heir was adopted by Horemheb and the early Ramessid rulers. The first of whom was appointed by Horemheb as prince regent during his lifetime, holding similar titles to those he had held under Tutankhamun. With Horemheb, a new dynasty began, the 19th. However, some evidence suggests that the Ramessid pharaohs considered him the true founder of the dynasty. During his short reign, which lasted barely one year, Sety, his son, was appointed vizier and commander of Sile. He also held several priestly titles, linking him to various gods worshipped in the Delta, including that of high priest of Seth.

Together with Hatshepsut's temple at Deir el-Bahri, which he restored, these buildings provided a splendid new setting for the annual Beautiful Feast of the Valley. During this festival, Amun of Karnak visited the gods of the west bank, and people came to pay their respects to the tombs of their deceased relatives, where they would eat, drink, and be merry in the company of their ancestors. Sety's building program was made possible by reopening several old quarries and mines, including those in Sinai. He also followed the practice of his predecessors by raiding Nubia to capture slaves who could be used as cheap labor. The result was a war with the Hittites, during which both vassal states were lost. A period of guarded peace followed.

It is unlikely that this man was ever more than a trusted military officer who perhaps conducted some campaigns instead of the king himself. However, Sety's successor Rameses II (1279–1213 BC), eager to stress his own role on the battlefield during the reign of his father, had Mehy's names and figures erased and in some cases replaced by his own as a crown prince. At the end of his reign, we do not know exactly when, he appointed his son and heir Rameses as co-regent while the latter was still a child. The sources for this co-regency all date back to Rameses' reign as sole king. He may have exaggerated its length and importance.

Although clearly a son of Sety I, he was almost certainly born during the reign of Horemheb, before his grandfather ascended the throne, and at a time when both Rameses I and Sety I were still simply high officials. A fact later emphasized rather than disguised by Rameses II himself in much the same way as Horemheb had done in his Coronation Text. The succession of the crown prince was not a foregone conclusion and had to be secured while his father was still alive. Reliefs in a small rock temple at Beit el-Wali commemorate the event. They depict the young king accompanied by two of his children: Amunherwenemef, the crown prince, and Khaemwaset, Rameses' fourth son. Despite being shown standing proudly in their chariots, they must have been mere striplings at the time.

The Battle of Qadesh was one of the most famous armed conflicts of antiquity. It is not as well-known because it was not significantly different from earlier battles, but rather because Rameses, despite being unable to achieve his goals, presented it at home as a huge victory. This victory was described in lengthy compositions that were part of an unprecedented propaganda campaign. The campaign's messages were carved on the walls of all major temples. Rameses had, therefore, made a quick advance to Qadesh with only one of his four divisions. He was then suddenly obliged to face the huge army that the Hittite king had mustered against him. Rameses declined a Hittite peace offer, although a truce was agreed.

In the 16th year of Rameses II's reign, Muwatalli's young son Urhi-Teshub, who had succeeded his father as Mursili III, was deposed by his uncle Hattusili III. Two years later, after some failed attempts to regain his throne with the help of the Babylonians and then the Assyrians, he finally fled to Egypt. The Assyrians had conquered Hanigalbat, a former vassal state that had recently defected to the Hittites. They were now threatening Carchemish and the Hittite empire itself. Although the Egyptians had to accept the loss of Qade sh and Amur ru, the peace brought a new stability on the northern front, and with the borders open to the Euphrates, the Black Sea, and the eastern Aegean, international trade soon flourished as it had not done since the days of Amenhotep III.

This Hittite princess was one of seven women who gained the status of 'great royal wife' during Rameses' reign, which lasted sixty-seven years. They were buried one after the other in a gigantic tomb in the Valley of the Kings (KV 5), recently rediscovered. During his long years on the throne, Rameses II carried out a vast building programme.

During the remainder of his reign, he gradually filled the country with his temples and statues, many of which he had taken from earlier rulers. There is hardly a site in Egypt where his cartouches are not found on the monuments. The series of eight rock temples in Lower Nubia, particularly impressive, includes two at Abu Simbel. Most were built with a workforce rounded up from local tribes, as attested by Wadi es-Sebua, which was constructed for the king by Setau, the viceroy of Nubia, after he had conducted a razzia in year 44. Among the hundreds of statues of deities and kings that Rameses usurped, those erected by Amenhotep III, the last king before the Amarna Period, were particularly favored, as were those made by the kings of the 12th Dynasty, the great rulers of the classical period of Egyptian history that served as a model for the new Egypt now taking shape, after the radical break in the tradition constituted by the Amarna Period.

It was perhaps because Ramessid scribes felt that these earlier works could not be equalled, let alone surpassed, that contemporary literature, such as love poetry and folk tales and mythic stories, sprang from an oral tradition. They were written not in classical Egyptian, but in the modern language first introduced in inscriptions by Akhenaten. This general decline in workmanship quality can be observed throughout the country, even in the king's own temples. Among two main relief-sculpting techniques, the superior but more time-consuming and expensive raised relief has largely disappeared after the first years of the reign, in favor of the common sunk relief. Amenhotep III had become deified during his three jubilees, but in this respect Rameses had less patience than his great predecessor. By his eighth year, we hear of a colossal statue being carved, which was given the name 'Rameses-the-god'.

Although as we have seen, the reign of Rameses II saw a marked revival of classical traditions, Khaemwaset must clearly have had a special interest in Egypt's glorious past. He restored several pyramids of Old Kingdom pharaohs in the Memphite necropolis, and in some of his own monuments attempted to copy the style of Old Kingdom tomb reliefs. By the time Khaemwaset was the eldest surviving son of his father's reign, he had become crown prince. He was likely in his sixties by then. A few years after that, he passed away, around the year 55. After Khaemwaset's death, Rameses II lived on for another twelve years until he finally died in the sixty-seventh year of his reign, making him the longest-reigning monarch since Pepy I (2321– 2287 BC) of the 6th Dynasty.

He became a living legend during the last years of his reign, and it was clear that he was greatly admired and envied by his successors. Merenptah's reign saw a significant event that occurred in his fifth year, as indicated by the victory stele, which details a campaign against the Libyans. They had been a problem even during his father's and grandfather's reigns, but the fortresses Rameses II had built along the western borders of the Nile delta were obviously unable to prevent the invasion of a massive coalition of Libyan and other tribes led by their king, Meren.

The Libyans were destined to fail on this occasion because, as Merenptah stated on his victory stele, their king Mereye had already been found guilty by the divine tribunal of Heliopolis. The god Atum, who presided over the tribunal, personally handed the sword of victory to Merenptah, making the battle a holy war. He must have realized that he did not have many years left. However, his mortuary temple on the Theban West Bank was constructed almost exclusively from blocks removed from earlier structures, particularly the nearby temples of Amenhotep III. After his death, succession troubles arose, as the next king, Sety II (1200-1194 BC), was likely the eldest son of Merenptah. However, a rival claimant, Amenmessu, briefly ruled for several years, primarily in the southern regions of the country. When Sety II died, his son Saptah succeeded him.

He was a young boy who suffered from an atrophied leg due to polio. His stepmother, Tausret, became the 'great royal wife' and acted as regent. The text is already grammatically correct and free of errors. She was the third queen of the New Kingdom to rule as pharaoh after Hatshepsut and Nefertiti.

The papyrus describes how a state of lawlessness and chaos broke out in Egypt due to forces from 'outside'. After several years with no ruler, a Syrian named Irsu seized power. He was a self-made man, meaning an upstart, who plundered the country along with his confederates. They treated the gods like ordinary humans and stopped sacrificing in the temples. This description bears resemblance to that of the Amarna Period during the years of restoration. After the death of Tausret, it is possible that Bay attempted to seize power and may have succeeded briefly before being ousted by Sethnakht. It has, therefore, been suggested that it represents the date of Sethnakht's victory and at the same time the true date of his accession. Having counted in retrospect the time it took him to overcome his adversaries, it is believed to be his first year.

He did not enjoy his newly gained kingship for long, for he died shortly after and was succeeded by his son Ramses III (1184–1153 BC). The new king inherited peace and stability from his father. However, he soon faced a multitude of challenges. He had to fight off further advances by Libyan tribes in year five. The tribes had used the period of internal struggle to penetrate into the western delta as far as the central Nile branch. The Egyptians had apparently accepted this peaceful immigration as inevitable. However, when a revolt broke out against the pharaoh due to his interference in the succession of their king, Rameses III swiftly responded and brought them back under Egyptian control.

The great battle against the Sea Peoples in year 8 was far more challenging than the previous conflicts. Their ultimate goal was clear: Egypt. In year 8 of Rameses III, they launched a combined land and sea attack on the Delta. Although the Sea Peoples had significantly altered the eastern Mediterranean world, they never succeeded in conquering Egypt. Their presence in Syria-Palestine did not initially appear to have affected Egypt's sway over its northern territories.

At home, Rameses III spent a lot of time and energy on his building projects, foremost of which was his large mortuary temple at Medinet Habu. It was begun shortly after his accession and finished by year 12; it still stands today as one of the best-preserved temples of the New Kingdom, featuring decoration on its exterior walls that include scenes from the battle with the Sea Peoples. It was closely modelled on the Ramesseum of his great predecessor Rameses II, whom Rameses III tried to emulate in many other ways. His own royal names were all but identical to those of Rameses II. He even named some of his sons after the latter's offspring. All was not well in Egypt, however, the situation had been deteriorating for some time.

Apparently, the plan was to murder the king during the annual Opet Festival in Thebes, but magical spells and wax figurines were also included in the preparations, which were smuggled into the harem. The plot must have failed, however, for the king's mummy shows no signs of a violent death, and his crown prince, Rameses IV, succeeded him instead of Pentaweret. They were probably all related to Rameses III.

During their reigns, Egypt lost control over its territories in Syria-Palestine, and the importance of Nubia was rapidly declining. Rameses IV was the fifth son of his father and had become crown prince by the end of his father's reign. None of his building plans came to fruition, however, for he died after a reign of five (perhaps seven) years, before he could complete any of them, despite his prayers on a large stele in Abydos asking Osiris to grant him a reign twice as long as the sixty-seven years of Ramses II.

During Rameses IV's reign, further delays in the delivery of basic commodities at Deir el-Medina occurred; at the same time, the influence of the high priest of Amun was growing. A major crime and corruption scandal among the priests at Elephantine, which had evolved during the reign of his father, is the main event known from his reign. He also oversaw the latter's mining activities in Timna and Sinai. After four years, Rameses V died of smallpox at a young age.

He took over the royal tomb and mortuary temple built by his nephew, whose burial was postponed until an alternative tomb could be found for him in Rameses VI's second year. It has been concluded by some researchers that the succession was accompanied by civil unrest. Entries in a necropolis journal indicate that the workers of Deir el-Medina, whose numbers were later reduced to sixty, stayed at home "for fear of the enemy." This does not seem very probable, however. The mere fact that most officials remained in office from one reign to the next is hardly enough proof against it. For instance, the same pattern was observed at the end of the 18th and 19th Dynasties, when there had been troubles.

During the reign of Pharaoh Rameses II (1279–1213 BC), grain prices rose to their peak and then declined. It is therefore hardly surprising that this period witnessed the first wave of tomb robbery. Known from a whole series of papyri, these records document the trials of the thieves who had been apprehended. The tombs in the Valley of the Kings were not involved. Only one 17th-Dynasty royal burial in Dra Abu el-Naga was robbed, along with several private tombs, and temple thefts were also being investigated.

Among the gifts received by Amenhotep were two hin of a costly ointment. Some 200 years earlier, during the reign of Horemheb, one of Maya's subordinates, a mere scribe of the treasury, had contributed four hin of the same ointment to the burial goods of his master. Rameses XI ruled for 30 years, with his power's geographical extent largely limited to Lower Egypt (the Nile Delta) during the final decade of his reign. During his reign, a crisis in the Theban area worsened, stemming from persistent trouble with Libyan gangs that prevented workers on the west bank from going to work. Famine, known as the 'year of the hyenas', further tomb robberies, temple thefts, and civil war also plagued the region.

The conflict quickly escalated, and over an eight-month period (between years 17 and 19), Panehesy and his troops besieged the high priest at Medinet Habu. In Thebes, General Piankh took over the titles of Panehesy and styled himself as vizier. Following Amenhotep's death, who may or may not have survived Panehesy's assault, Piankh also became high priest of Amun, uniting the three highest offices of the country in one person. With Piankh's military coup, the Wehem Mesut, or 'renaissance', begins. This term was also used by kings at the start of the 12th and 19th Dynasties to signify Egypt's rebirth following a period of turmoil.

Herihor, Piankh's son-in-law, succeeded him in all his roles upon Piankh's death. Following Rameses XI's demise, Herihor also claimed royal authority. After Rameses III, the Egyptians finally lost their provinces in Palestine and Syria. This occurred following the invasion of the Sea Peoples and the disappearance of the Hittite empire, which had broken up into several small states. During the Renaissance, Piankh and his successors, assisted by the descendants of Deir elMedina's workmen who now lived at Medinet Habu, began to tap a different source of gold and precious stones: the very same tombs in the Valley of the Kings that their fathers and grandfathers had carved and decorated, as well as many other royal and private tombs in Theban necropolis.

What was worse, however, is that in the eyes of all but a few of the Amar na élite, he had actually wrecked society. We have already seen how burial customs after the Amar na Period reflect a totally different attitude towards the king, as a reaction against the way Akhenaten had tried to monopolize the funerary beliefs of his subjects. This monopoly was not limited to life in the hereafter but also deeply affected life on earth.

Traditionally, access to the god's cult image in the temple was restricted to the king and the professional priesthood representing him. For the vast majority of the population, the only means of getting in contact with their home town's gods, without state or official temple cult intervention, lay during regularly held processions. On these occasions, images of the gods were carried from one temple to another on the occasion of a religious festival. In a traditional representative theocracy, the gods embodied the cosmic order they had created at the beginning of time. The king, as their intermediary, represented the gods on earth, maintaining cosmic order through temple rituals and carrying out their will through his government.

After the Amarna period, the problem of the unity and plurality of the gods was solved in a different way: Amun-Ra became the universal, transcendent god, who existed far away, independent of his creation; the other gods and goddesses were aspects of him, they were his immanent manifestations. The universal god had become the true king. Although the pharaoh's traditional titles, rooted in mythology and expressing his divinity, remained unchanged, he had actually become more human than ever before in Egyptian history. Texts express this by bridging the gap between being far away and being nearby: “He is far away as one who sees, and near as one who hears.” Amun-R, the god, looked down upon his worshippers from afar. At the same time, he was present in their lives through the manifestation of his will, as he heard their prayers and intervened divinely.

This new form of religious experience, usually referred to as 'personal piety', was entirely characteristic of the Ramessid period. Its origins, however, were suppressed by Akhenaten and dated back to the mid-18th dynasty. These penitential hymns attributed illness (often blindness, although this word is probably used metaphorically) to the state of being guilty of a hidden sin. Once revealed in the text on a votive stele, it was no longer hidden, so that God would 'return' to his worshipper and make him see again. The gods are also said to have abandoned Egypt.

Oracles, which had originally been consulted only by the king, perhaps as early as the Old Kingdom, were used during the 18th Dynasty to seek the god's approval for a king's accession or major trade and military expeditions. In the Ramessid Period, they began to be used to consult the god on all sorts of matters in the lives of ordinary human beings. Appointments, disputes over property, accusations of crimes, and later even questions seeking God's reassurance that one would safely live on in the hereafter, were thus subject to God's will. The mummies of their royal ancestors were no longer considered the former incarnations of God on earth, and so, with little scruple, their tombs could be robbed and their bodies unwrapped.

It is also clear that neither Nubian nor Syro-Palestinian origins were viewed as disadvantageous factors in terms of an individual's status or career prospects, especially in the cosmopolitan climate of the New Kingdom, where Asiatic religious cults and technological advancements were widely accepted. The Iconography of Warfare and Conquest: Textual and Visual Evidence

The term 'Nine Bows' was often used to refer to the enemies of Egypt. The specific identity of these adversaries varied over time, but they typically consisted of Asiatics and Nubians. There were, therefore, several depictions in Greek-Roman temples showing lines of gods capturing birds, wild animals, and foreigners in clamps.

The rekhyt-bir d (a type of lapwing or plover with a distinctive crested head) was often used as a symbol for foreign captives or subject peoples. This usage is likely due to its physical appearance, which resembles a bound captive, with its wings pinned behind its back. The conquests of the Rekhyt appear to represent the conquered peoples of northern Egypt during a critical period when the country was unified under a single ruler. The pharaohs were essentially responsible for extending Egypt's borders, as their royal names and titles suggested a vast area of political control.

The first cataract, located just a short distance to the south, presented a significant challenge to Nile boats. As a result, all trade goods had to be transported along the riverbank. The land route to the east of the Nile was protected by a massive mud-brick wall. 5 km A 12th-Dynasty construction The border with Nubia had shifted much farther south by the 12th Dynasty, located at the Semna gorge, which is the narrowest point in the Nile Valley. Although Egypt was clearly self-sufficient in a wide diversity of rocks, plants, and animals, there were nevertheless many highly prized materials that were not obtainable within the Nile Valley itself.

Few of these vessels have been identified with pottery from contemporary sites in Palestine, suggesting they were types specifically produced for export. A rock carving at the Lower Nubian site of Gebel Sheikh Suleiman is now on display in the Khartoum Museum. It appears to record a 1st-Dynasty campaign as far south as the second cataract, suggesting that contacts with the A Group had become more militaristic by this date. A process of severe poverty appears to have taken place in Lower Nubia during the 1st Dynasty, likely as a direct result of the early Egyptian economic exploitation of the region. Their way of life seemed to have been dominated by cattle herding, with a social system that was likely tribal in nature until they were integrated into Egyptian society.

The scenes also show myrrh trees being loaded onto ships so that the Egyptians could produce their own aromatics from them. It has been argued that this in itself may be an argument for the combined Nile-overland route from Punt to Egypt, given that such plants might well have died during the more difficult voyage northwards along the Red Sea coast. Opinions differ on which of these territories were politically or socially colonized, or if the situation was more erratic, with periods of raids aimed at safeguarding trade routes and providing supplies of war booty. The storage space in the fortresses was also utilized to store materials and products imported by the Egyptians as they traveled to Thebes or Itjtawy.

There is very little evidence of a permanent Egyptian presence in Palestine during the Middle Kingdom. There were certainly contacts between Egypt and the Levant as well as the Aegean during the 12th and 13th Dynasties. However, it remains unclear to what extent Egypt gained political or economic control over any part of the eastern Mediterranean. A fragment of Amenemhat II's annals preserved at Memphis records at least two invasions of the Levant during his reign. The stele of Khusobek, housed at Manchester Museum, documents an expedition launched against the Palestinian city of Shechem in the reign of Senusret III.

A reasonable archaeological case can be made for a fairly strong and continuous Middle Kingdom Egyptian economic presence in Palestine and Byblos, as evidenced by the periodic reinforcement of military pressure. Egyptian activities in the Levant during the New Kingdom are documented through both archaeological and textual evidence.

The principal debate concerning Egyptian involvement in Syria-Palestine during the New Kingdom centers on the question of the degree to which Egypt maintained a permanent military and/or civilian presence at the various towns and cities it had conquered. Other scholars argue that the material culture of Egyptian sites in the eastern Delta is clearly distinct from that of the nearest towns in Palestine, just on the other side of Sinai. It seems highly unlikely that many Egyptians ever lived among local populations (in contrast to the extensive architectural and arte-factual evidence of Egyptians colonizing Nubia during the New Kingdom). Throughout the Middle and New kingdoms, the essentially xenophobic ideology continued, portraying stereotypical barbaric Nubians as worthless representatives of chaos in official art and literature.

Foreigners, including Nubians and Asiatics, lived happily alongside native Egyptians in many towns in Egypt. This contrasts with a deliberate New Kingdom policy of acculturation in both Nubia and the Levant, where local élite were encouraged to adopt Egyptian customs. Their children were sometimes forcibly removed to be educated in Egypt, only to return home fully indoctrinated with the Egyptian way of life. The overall image of Egyptian 'imperialism' is multifaceted. The economic and political pragmatism of the pharaohs was often cloaked in the hyperbole of royal rhetoric and piety. The debate between ideology versus economics is difficult to resolve because we rely primarily on a combination of royal, religious, and funerary texts for our reconstruction of Egyptian behavior in the outside world. However, the real story probably lies in the more prosaic archival material that has so rarely survived.

Egyptian objects featured ivory, ebony, and gold. Locally crafted items used alternative materials and were produced in a lesser skilled manner. The archaeological evidence suggests a peak of Egypto-Byblian contact in the 19th Dynasty, followed by decline in the 20th and 21st Dynasties. The Tale of Wenamun, a quasi-historical description, documents an expedition to Byblos during the late 20th-Dynasty period. A resurgence of links is observed in the 22nd and 23rd Dynasties. Byblos' significance after the Third Intermediate Period began to wane in favor of its neighboring ports, Tyre and Sidon.

Some groups, such as the Denen, Lukka, and Sherden, were already active by the reign of Akhenaten (1352-1336 BC). Members of the Lukka, Sherden, and Peleset are portrayed as mercenaries fighting for the army of Rameses II (1279-1213 BC) at the Battle of Qadesh. Later in the Ramessid Period, the Sea Peoples are described and depicted on reliefs at Medinet Habu and Karnak. They are also mentioned in the Great Harris Papyrus, a list of temple endowments during the reign of Rameses III (1184–1153 BC). It was not, however, simply a matter of importing goods into Egypt.

Centralized government was replaced by political fragmentation and the re-emergence of local centers of power. A substantial influx of non-Egyptians (Libyans and Nubians) permanently modified the population's profile, while Egypt as a whole became more inward-looking, with its contacts with the outside world and impact on the Levant greatly reduced in scale. Careful collation of the Manethonian lists with scattered inscriptions of kings and local dignitaries from the period has yielded a chronology that is accepted by most scholars in its main points, but some areas remain subjects for debate. Notably, the relationships and spheres of influence of certain provincial rulers who adopted royal status during the late ninth and eighth centuries BC are still a matter of discussion. With the exception of sites like Tanis, evidence from this period in the Delta is relatively scarce. Thebes has yielded a significant number of artefacts, but private statuary and funerary equipment dominate, while economic sources such as administrative papyri are extremely rare.

While this connection may have been a factor in the new ruler's rise to power, the growing influence of the cult of Amun and its officials was undoubtedly also significant. During this period, the government of Egypt was effectively a theocracy, with supreme political authority vested in the god Amun himself. The same principles apply to the north as well. Smendes and Tentamun are described in Wenamun as 'the pillars which Amun has set up for the north of his land'. The city of Tanis was developed as a northern counterpart to Thebes, Amun's principal cult center.

There is evidence that Memphis functioned as a residence of the northern kings. A decree of Smendes is recorded as having been issued there, and the ancient city may once again serve as a major administrative center. Plan of the remains of the temples and tombs at the Easter Delta site of Tanis. The activities of the northern rulers during the 21st Dynasty are poorly documented. The long-term control of Upper Egypt subsequently passed to the family of General Piankh's colleague, Herihor.

Although the Tanite kings' temporal authority was formally recognized throughout Egypt, and Theban commanders made only limited claims to royal status, it was nonetheless they who effectively controlled Middle and Upper Egypt. The principal activity documented in the south during the 21st Dynasty was the systematic dismantling of the New Kingdom's royal burials in the Theban necropolis. After the reigns of Smendes and his successor Amenemnisu (1043–1039 BC), the throne in the north passed to Psusennes I, son of the Theban commander Pinudjem I, and overlord of Upper Egypt to his brother Menkheperre.

Although major incursions by Meshwesh and Libu had been repulsed by Merenptah and Rameses III, the settlement of immigrants, war captives, and garrison troops, particularly in the Delta region and between Memphis and Herakleopolis, has led some to suggest that by the end of the New Kingdom, the Egyptian army was almost entirely composed of Libyan mercenaries. The decentralization of government during the 21st Dynasty facilitated the growth of provincial power bases, and local dynasties of Libyan chiefs, descended from settlers of the late New Kingdom, were able to increase their autonomy. The ruling families in both the north and south during the 21st Dynasty included individuals with distinctly Libyan names. Given some form of acculturation was likely practiced, many more are probably recorded under Egyptian names. It was therefore only the culmination of an established trend. At the end of the 21st Dynasty, the throne in Tanis passed to the chief of the Meshwesh, Sheshonq I (King Sheshonq I, 945–924 BC).

The transfer of power from Psusennes II appears to have been accomplished with a minimum of opposition—it was undoubtedly eased by the fact that Sheshonq was the nephew of the earlier Tanite king Osork I, while his own son, Osork I (924–889 BC), was married to Psusennes II's daughter Maatkare. Sheshonq I intervened aggressively in the politics of the Levant after more than a century of inaction by Egyptian rulers to reassert pharaonic dominance. If this was intended as the first stage of a program to re-establish Egyptian authority in Palestine, it remained only a fleeting moment.

Sheshonq died soon after his return to Egypt. Under his successors, relations with the Levant appeared to have reverted to purely commercial contacts, notably the reopening of relations with Byblos. During the reign of Sheshonq III (825–773 BC), numerous local rulers, particularly in the Delta, became virtually autonomous and declared themselves kings. While some local kings are assigned to the 23rd Dynasty by scholars, it remains unclear whether they should be equated with the '23rd Dynasty' as recorded by Manetho. The latter may have been composed of successors to the 22nd Dynasty at Tanis.

During the second half of the eighth century BC, the rulers of Kush emerged as strong contenders for power over Egypt. After this show of strength, Piy returned to Nubia, leaving the political situation in Egypt virtually unchanged. Over the next decade, Tefnakht assumed the throne, and his successor Bakenrenef (Bocchoris) ruled as part of the 24th Dynasty.

Although based at Sais, Bakenrenef's authority was soon acknowledged throughout the Nile Delta and as far south as Hermopolis. In the early 7th century BC, intervention in the politics of this region led to a direct confrontation with the power of Assyria, which was expanding its control over the Levant. Finally, after the sack of Thebes by Ashurbanipal in 663 BC, the last Kushite monarch was permanently expelled from Egypt. It was left to Psamtek of Sais (who had been installed by the Assyrians as a vassal ruler) to recover Egypt's independence.

The 21st to 24th Dynasties: Libyan Period

The Libyans who settled in Egypt before and during the Third Intermediate Period were predominantly drawn from the Meshwesh (or Ma) and the Libu, the principal groups that had threatened Egypt's security during the New Kingdom. Their homeland appeared to be Cyrenaica, where they followed an economy based mainly on pastoral nomadism, although there was also some evidence for settlements. Although increased contact with foreign lands and customs during the New Kingdom had made Egypt a cosmopolitan society with a mixed population, foreign settlers still adopted many aspects of Egyptian culture, including their names, clothing, and burial practices.

Their distinctive and very un-Egyptian names—Osorkon, Sheshonq, Takelot, and others—survived for centuries after the arrival of the Libyans in Egypt, where as in earlier periods foreigners usually adopted or were given Egyptian names within one or two generations. Libyan chiefdom titles were retained long after settlement in Egypt. A feather worn in the hair remained as a distinguishing mark for chiefs of the Meshwesh and Libu. It is, therefore, very likely that several of the major changes in the administration, society, and culture of Egypt that occurred during this period may have had their origins in this mixing of societies.

The most characteristic feature of Egypt during the Third Intermediate Period is the political fragmentation of the country. The situation in Upper Egypt was similar, although this part of the country retained greater territorial cohesion than the north. During the First and Second Intermediate periods, the division of power between two or more rulers was viewed as unacceptable. In contrast, during the Third Intermediate Period, decentralization was not consistently seen as a negative phenomenon.

In favor of this interpretation, it should be noted that—despite the prominence of military titles and fortified settlements during this period—the explicit references to internal conflict are limited and should not be interpreted as signs of a slide into anarchy. The semiautonomous status of centers such as Bubastis, Mendes, Sebennytos, and Diospolis was likely established during the early phase of Libyan settlement, and was maintained throughout the subsequent centuries. While centers such as Hermopolis, Herakleopolis, el-Hiba, and Abydos were significant, Thebes maintained its pre-eminent position throughout the Third Intermediate Period.

Later, the southern rulers formed an alliance with Kush. To date, inscriptions from the reigns of the Kushite monarchs have survived, even after their expulsion, and continue to be found as late as the early years of Psamtek I (664-610 BC). After the New Kingdom, the evolution of the hieratic script used in business documents produced two distinct forms: demotic in the north and 'abnormal' hieratic at Thebes. This suggests that the administration of the north had little to no influence on Thebes. The relationship between Amun and the king changed during the late New Kingdom. Indeed, while three of the Theban priests adopted kingly titles, the pharaoh Psusennes I also appeared as high priest of Amun, indicating that the offices were closer to equality than ever before. The Thebans' assumption of royal attributes was restricted. Although Herihor and Pinudjem I were depicted with kingly prerogatives (equal in stature to the gods), they were adorned with royal costume, and their names appeared in cartouches. However, Herihor's royal prenomen is merely the title 'high priest of Amun'. The text appears to be incomplete, and it's unclear what the original content was supposed to be. Without a complete and coherent text, it's impossible for me to correct and enhance it. It was first the high priests at Thebes, followed by the "god's wives of Amun" and their officials, who held power.

The Libyans were never fully Egyptianized, despite their pharaonic trappings. The kings preferred different patterns of rule to those of their New Kingdom predecessors. The titles of the Libyan rulers are characterized by a monotonous repetition of prenomina and royal epithets, which frequently hinders the correct attribution of royal monuments from this period. The power structure in Egypt around 730 BC, as revealed by the 'Victory Stele' of Piye, shows chiefs of the Meshwesh on a footing of equality with kings, although without royal titles.

During the New Kingdom, donations were undertaken only by the king. In the Third Intermediate Period, numerous stelae recorded temple donations, with the donor often being a Libyan chief or private individual. After the New Kingdom, military power rather than bureaucratic control was a major basis of authority in Egypt during the Libyan period. Evidence suggests that a number of fortresses were constructed in Upper Egypt during the 21st Dynasty, with notable examples under Pharaohs Pinudjem I and Menkheperre. It was a frontier fort and the northernmost headquarters of the Upper Egyptian rulers during the 21st Dynasty.

The administration of the Theban west bank sought refuge in the fortified temple enclosure of Medinet Habu during the troubles at the end of the New Kingdom. It is likely that this location remained the residence of the high priests throughout the 21st Dynasty. The rebellion in Theban occurred during the reign of Pinudjem I, but its nature was obscure. The economy and control of resources during the 21st-24th Dynasties are notable. This period stands out for its relative lack of large-scale royal stone monuments, unlike those seen in the New Kingdom.

The Third Intermediate Period commenced during a time of economic hardship. Revenues from the Levant and the African interior were significantly lower than those available during the New Kingdom. Egypt's economy showed signs of improvement during this time. The unambitious nature of royal building projects and their high dependence on reused materials during the Third Intermediate Period can be plausibly explained by the politically fragmented state of the country.

The majority of these artifacts date back to the 22nd and 23rd Dynasties. They document the allocation of land to temples with the aim of establishing endowments for funerary cults. The distribution of these stelae suggests that substantial areas in the western delta and central delta were recently brought under cultivation. The burial goods of the 22nd-Dynasty kings found in the royal tombs at Tanis included substantial quantities of gold and silver. An inscription from Bubastis, recording the dedication by Osork on I of statues and cult utensils to the temples of Egypt, lists the equivalent of over 391 tons of gold and silver objects—all apparently presented during the first four years of the king's reign.

A portion of this is believed to have originated from the spoils of Sheshonq I's Palestinian campaign several years ago, with some of it possibly being recycled material taken from New Kingdom tombs. The dismantling of the New Kingdom royal burials at Thebes during the 21st Dynasty was likely due to this principle rather than a pious respect for the deceased. The hieratic dockets on coffins and shrouds that record these actions show that they were carried out on the authority of the ruling generals. Hundrëds of rock graffiti written by the necropolis scribe Butehamun and his colleagues testify to the systematic searching out and clearing of old tombs.

Curiously, what may have begun as a prerogative of the Theban rulers alone was soon widespread; in the 21st Dynasty, a high proportion of coffins used for burials at Thebes were reinscribed and reused within a short time of the original burial—probably illicitly. A docket written on a coffin in the British Museum records its restoration to the true owner after necropolis workers had been caught in the act of usurping it. Although the suggestion that Lower Nubia was depopulated during this period is likely an exaggeration, the population may have been less prosperous than in earlier times and possibly reverted to a semi-nomadic economy or migrated to the more prosperous south. A clear evolution in the arrangements for the burials is apparent.

During the New Kingdom, this was the center of the cult of Amun in Nubia, and the worship of the state god of Egypt became a defining feature of the Kushite ruling elite. Piy, however, showed great respect for the religious traditions of Egypt and ensured that its temples were safeguarded against looting and desecration. The interment of a team of chariot horses, a feature also associated with the burials of Piy's successors, was quite un-Egyptian. This distinctly Kushite practice was evident in the region.

In the north, however, the local dynasts were left in control of their provinces. Under Tefnakht of Sais and his successor Bakenrenef, the 25th Dynasty resumed its territorial expansion. The young Taharqo was present in person at the Battle of Eltekeh in 701 BC. A stele from Dahshur records details of a rigorous military exercise conducted by the same king in the desert, between Memphis and the Faiyum. The post of high priest of Amun, which had been a source of tension in previous years, had apparently remained vacant for nearly eight centuries but was now reinstated and assigned to a king's son.

Significantly, however, the holder wielded little or no civil or military power. During the early period of Kushite rule, Nubian retainers from the royal household were appointed to key positions in the civil and religious administration at Thebes. However, they were soon replaced after a few years by members of local families. Small but significant changes were made to royal iconography. A double uraeus was regularly depicted on the king's head, and the blue crown ceased to be shown. Instead, the cap crown became common in depictions, both in its basic form and with additional bands—a distinctly Kushite headgear.

The 25th Dynasty was ruled by Piy and his brother Shabitqo, who reigned from 702 to 690 BC. Despite these divergence from Egyptian norms, however, the Kushite rulers sought to strengthen their legitimacy by posing as champions of ancient tradition. Scenes in temple T at Kawa were copied by Memphite artists from Old Kingdom royal mortuary temples at Saqqara and Abusir. The inclusion of a scene depicting Taharqo as a sphinx defeating Libyan foes, although based on Old Kingdom models, may have been intended to emphasize the Kushites' triumph over Egypt's former rulers. These tendencies developed further after the intensification of contacts during the 8th century, and by the time of Kashta, a strong Egyptianization of the ruler was apparent in iconography.

This acculturation remained a key component of Kushite culture for centuries after the Nubians had relinquished control of Egypt. Although buried in their homeland, the rulers constructed tombs in the Egyptian style, each with a pyramid structure, an offering chapel to the east, and a vaulted burial chamber adorned with scenes and texts from the New Kingdom repertoire of 'books of the netherworld'. Distinctive un-Egyptian names, such as Irigadiganen and Kelbask en, also mark out several other officials of the period as Kushites. Some took Egyptian names, while others retained their original Nubian names.

The cultural exchange, however, was almost entirely a one-way process. Very little of the Kushite culture was adopted into Egyptian material culture, and what was taken over was not retained permanently. The characteristic regalia of the Kushite rulers disappeared after the 25th Dynasty. Other innovations, such as the occasional depiction of the goddesses Isis and Nephthys, also ceased. They were typically shown with a "Nubian" hairstyle on funerary monuments. This association was part of a broader and deeply rooted revival of things past that affected many aspects of Egypt's culture, religion, script, literature, art, architecture, and burial practices during the first millennium BC. The use of ancient cultures as a source of inspiration for modern art and creativity is not a new phenomenon. It has been a recurring feature in Egyptian culture throughout its history.

In this instance, it had its origins in the later Libyan period, dating back to the first half of the 8th century BC. The late 22nd and 23rd Dynasties saw a progressive simplification in royal titles. Imitation of Old and Middle Kingdom models started to appear in royal iconography and funerary practices. The 26th Dynasty marked a significant period when archaism accelerated, particularly during the late eighth and early seventh centuries. The rise of archaism in the 8th and 7th centuries likely stemmed from the desire of foreign rulers to be perceived as Egyptian.

This was likely one of the primary methods by which older models were transmitted. The possibility exists that 'pattern books', copied repeatedly over the centuries, may also have played a role. Direct slavish copying was, however, not common. This blending of different influences is apparent even within individual works: statues of Taharqo and Tanutamani (664–656 BC) from Gebel Barkal have bodies modeled strongly after those of the Old Kingdom, while their torsos display a median line characteristic of sculptures created in the Middle Kingdom.

Kush and Assyria Although centralized government had not been restored in Egypt by the Kushite monarchs, their authority as overlords enabled them to adopt a more active policy with regard to the Levant than had any of the Libyan kings since Sheshonq I. This led to conflict with Assyria, whose forces had taken over Babylon and parts of the eastern Mediterranean coast in the 8th century BC. Although Kushite interference in Palestine was intended to lead ultimately to the Assyrian conquest of Egypt, a threat to the country's independence certainly existed. The Kushite rulers, although maintaining nominal claims to authority over Egypt for several generations, were never able to put them into effect afterwards.

The bloodshed and destruction that followed from the Kushite opposition to Assyria proved to be a cloud with a silver lining. It emphasized the necessity for military and civil cooperation among the rulers of the principalities, if independence was to be regained. This cooperation brought an exceptional individual to power who possessed the resources and abilities to liberate Egypt and lead it into a new phase. During his long reign, he threw off the Assyrian yoke and succeeded where the Kushites had failed, in reuniting the whole of Egypt under his sole command. It is only at this point that the Third Intermediate Period can be said to have ended, with Egypt poised once again to reap the benefits of a centralized government controlled by a strong king.

Religion and material culture in the Third Intermediate Period. While there was considerable continuity in the practice of the temple cult during the pharaonic period, two key factors distinguished its performance in this period: the diminished importance of the king and the growing prominence of women in cult activities. The culmination of this trend was the unprecedented prominence of the high priest of Amun during the 21st-22nd dynasties, at which period his power was augmented by both civil and military authority. However, as has been noted above, the excessive influence of this individual had a destabilizing effect on the country. In the eighth century BC, religious authority in Thebes became increasingly centered on the 'god's wife of Amun', while civil and military powers were distributed among others.

While the precise religious significance of this office is unclear, it's not a coincidence that these high-ranking women also held titles related to the importance of goddesses such as Mut and Hathor. Each of them was believed to be instrumental in Amun's perpetuating the creative process, which ultimately ensured the continuation of the cosmos. The post of 'chief of the musical troupe' disappeared during the 22nd Dynasty, and in its place, there was a major development of the office of 'God's Wife of Amun' (or Divine Adoratrice). There was undoubtedly a political dimension to this.

The rise of the 'god's wife' coincided with the decline of the power of the high priest of Amun. This may have been promoted as a measure to address the 'problem' of Theban secessionism, since the 'god's wife' allowed the distant royal house to be represented at Thebes. However, her celibacy meant that no subdynasty could arise, as successors were adopted instead. The status of the 'god's wife' continued to evolve and the adoption system remained in place until the end of the 26th Dynasty. The importance of the 'god's wife' during the Third Intermediate Period becomes evident: from the 23rd Dynasty onwards, her status parallels that of the king, and by the 25th Dynasty, she is depicted with increased prominence on monuments.

Among their many titles, these ladies were known as 'nurses' or 'divine mothers' of these gods. The Third Intermediate Period marked the early stages in the growth of emphasis on the mother-child relationship in Egyptian religion, which would become a pervading aspect of life in Egypt during the remainder of the first millennium BC. A major manifestation of this 'mammisiac' religion is the increasing importance attached to divine triads, with the child-god (identifiable with the king) as the offspring of two other deities. Burial practices in ancient Egypt during this period are amply reflected in the manner of treatment accorded to the dead. While the practice is most conspicuous for kings, it also extended to persons of high rank—the high priests at Memphis, whose tombs were constructed on the edge of the precinct of the temple of Ptah; Queen Khamun, buried at Leontopolis near Bubastis; a high official buried adjacent to the enclosure wall of the temple at Tell el-Balamun.

The burial site of 'King Harsie Se' and later 'god's wives' at Medinet Habu may also have been influenced by local cult activities. During the Third Intermediate Period, the Small Temple there became closely associated with the 'Mound of Djeme', where rituals relating to the creative powers of Amun took place. The gathering of the New Kingdom royal mummies and the 21st Dynasty priests of Amun into caches in older tombs dates back to the eleventh and tenth centuries BC, marking the beginning of a new pattern. During the 21st Dynasty, elite burials at Thebes utilized the Book of the Dead. Additionally, they incorporated the Amduat and Litany of Ra into their non-royal repertoire. However, by the 22nd Dynasty, these traditions had begun to decline.

Burial practices also lend support to the notion of a north-south division in the population and material culture of Egypt during this period. Although Delta sites, apart from Tanis, have yielded few burials datable to these centuries, evidence from the Memphite and Faiyum areas can be compared with the more abundant material from the south. The introduction of northern burial practices in Upper Egypt appears to have coincided with the imposition of stronger royal authority over the south during the reigns of Sheshonq I and his successors. During the following period, northern and southern styles of coffin emerged, likely reflecting the country's gradual modernization and possibly mirroring the social divisions suggested by other evidence.

A revised Book of the Dead in the new so-called Saite recension was inscribed on papyri and coffins. The archaizing fervor of the period led to the copying of passages from the Pyramid Texts and their addition to the current repertoire. Thebes appears to have been a major center for these innovations, which spread northwards during the seventh century BC. Comparable developments may have occurred in other areas, but the local chronology at sites like Memphis is less clear. Royal statues in stone are particularly rare. Those of the 21st Dynasty kings were often taken from earlier rulers. Although original works were produced during the 22nd and 23rd Dynasties, most of the surviving examples are of modest size. During the 22nd to 25th Dynasties, a significant number of statues of officials were dedicated in temples. Some of these statues are of exceptionally fine work.

The fine reliefs of Sheshonq I from el-Hiba and of Osorkon II from Bubastis demonstrate that two-dimensional work of high quality was still being produced, despite the subject matter of the scenes being largely derivative. The silver coffins of King Psusennes I and King Sheshonq II, along with a wide range of gold and silver vessels and jewelry from the Tanite royal tombs, testify to the expertise of Egyptian metalworkers. Although foreign influence is occasionally apparent in the shapes and decoration of vessels. Of greater significance was the huge expansion of the range and technical excellence of metal sculpture that occurred during this period, some of it in gold and silver, but the major part in bronze.

Small bronze deity figures were frequent, and now began the tradition of small statuettes that led to the production of thousands of examples during the succeeding centuries. The figure of Karomama, 'god's wife', in the Louvre is the supreme example of this type, although a series of Osiris statues may have been equally imposing in their original form.

Magical figurines were equally typical of the period. Designed to provide protection during childbirth, they offered nourishment for the young. These blue-green faience objects often featured spots and details in brown. Typically, they depicted the household god Bes, a monk-like figure, or a nude female holding a vase or musical instrument, or sometimes suckling. Although they occur as far south as El Kurru in Nubia, the concentration of finds at northeast Delta sites suggests that this was their primary area of production. Conclusion: The pejorative implications of the term 'intermediate' do little justice to the developments that took place in Egypt between 1069 and 664 BC. Although the power structure within the country was very different from that which existed in the New Kingdom, the towns and cities of Egypt flourished, and the economy of the country was generally healthy.

By 660 BC Psamtek had control of the entire Delta, and from this powerful military base, he was able to gain mastery of the rest of the country by 656 BC. Mainly, it would seem, he achieved this through diplomatic means, although the wheels of diplomacy were certainly facilitated by the availability of a substantial well-equipped military force comprised of foreign mercenaries who were not overly scrupulous. The native Egyptian warrior class, known as the machimoi, were originally from Libya and posed a significant internal threat to royal authority. It's worth noting that these troops were not consistently reliable. There is evidence of a revolt by mercenaries at Elephantine during the reign of Apries (589-570 BC).

A small recovery seems to have been made in the reign of Psamtek II, who was able to mount an expedition into Palestine during the fourth year of his reign. He defeated a Chaldaean invasion of Egypt in the fourth year of his reign. After this, the Chaldaeans faced sufficient problems within their empire that kept them fully occupied for the early part of his reign. The south was not as great a threat as the north, but the Nubians could not be ignored, not least because they had certainly not given up their ambitions to rule Egypt.

It appears that the dynasty's military operations to the south were largely unsuccessful. However, a demotic papyrus from the reign of Ahmose II describes a small expedition sent by the king into Nubia, whose character is unclear. Archaeological evidence also confirms the presence of an Egyptian garrison at Dorginarti in Lower Nubia during the Saite and Persian periods. Relations with the Libyans were not consistently positive during the Saite Dynasty. Xerxes (486–465 BC), like some other Persian kings, did not exhibit the same level of sensitivity towards temple privileges. He was particularly criticized for his disrespect towards these sacred institutions.

The Persians, like the Ptolemies after them, recognized that the Egyptian system for governing the country was the most effective and adopted it, adding only the necessary minimal Persian administrative layer to integrate the province into the Achaemenid empire. This contingent performed particularly well at Artemisium, where it captured five Greek ships with their crews. However, this record does not appear to have been maintained at Salamis. The Great King was often portrayed as pharaoh for ideological reasons, but he was actually a landlord based in Iran who could not help but be seen by many as a mere token pharaoh.

In 404 BC, the younger Amyrtaios successfully raised the flag of revolt, inaugurating the last prolonged period of independence under native rulers that Phoenician civilization would eventually enjoy. This had no effect on him, as Teos was removed from power under circumstances that have been vividly described. If we consider the titles of the rulers of the 29th Dynasty, we find that Nepherites I bears a Horus name borrowed from Psamtek I and a Golden Horus name taken from Ahmose II. Similarly, Hakor uses the Horus and nebty names of Psamtek I and the Golden Horus name of Ahmose II.

The ease with which this policy could be conducted is explained by the fact that it unfolded as part of a much greater game. This Egyptian activity took place against the backdrop of the struggle for independence of other western provinces of the Achaemenid empire and the long-standing rivalry between Sparta and Persia to define their respective spheres of influence in the Aegean, Asia Minor, and the eastern Mediterranean. Artaxerxes III (343–338 BC) launched three significant campaigns to reclaim the lost and perilous province. We therefore find that Hakor put together a large force of such troops in the 380s BC, and Teos employed 10,000 picked mercenaries in 361/0 BC. Nectanebo II is said to have had 20,000 when Artaxerxes III invaded the country in 343/2 BC. The troops demonstrated clear superiority over the native Egyptian militia in the civil war between Nectanebo II and Teos. However, they proved unreliable during the successful Persian invasion of Egypt in 343 BC. Plutarch describes them at one point in somewhat disparaging terms as ‘a rabble of artisans whose inexperience made them worthy of nothing but contempt’. However, they were certainly capable of effective military action. Diodorus comments on the effectiveness of their skirmishing tactics against the forces of Artaxerxes in 374/3 BC. In the civil war of 360 BC, they initially performed well against Agesilaus and Nectanebo II, but were ultimately outgeneraled and outfought by their Greek opponents.

Nectanebo I fortified the coast and the north-east Delta extensively in 374/3 BC. All entrances were blocked off by land and sea: at each of the seven mouths there was a town with large towers and a wooden bridge dominating the entrance. Pelusium had a ditch around it with fortified points of access by water that were blocked by moles, and all the land approaches were flooded. The town at the Mendesian mouth had both a surrounding wall and a fort inside. The Egyptians' expertise in this area is evident in their operations against Agesilaus and Nectanebo in 360 BC, and in the measures taken by Nectanebo II to counter the assault of Artaxerxes III in 343/2 BC. Too often, however, it was the high command of the Egyptian army that proved its Achilles' heel. Jealousy between Egyptian and foreign generals became an easy flashpoint. The Egyptians needed to counter both Persian fleet movements and those of forces coming from the south by land.

Even beyond this specific context, it should be remembered that the possession of effective naval units greatly strengthened the strategic and tactical mobility of Egyptian forces in the eastern Mediterranean theater. Fleets were a frequent subject of comment in our sources. For instance, in 400 BC, we find a rebel Persian admiral named Tamos. Taking refuge in Egypt with his fleet, he was promptly murdered by an enigmatic Egyptian ruler (probably Amyrtaios) specifically to gain possession of his naval assets. In 361/0, a substantial fleet is prepared alongside the army to participate in the general revolt of the western provinces of the Persian empire. Although the Egyptian ships are often described as triremes, it is worth noting that the Persian fleet assembled for operations against Egypt in 374 BC consisted of 300 triremes and 200 triakontors (single-bank galleys with thirty oars). The Egyptian navy likely also included such lighter units.

It comes as no surprise that when Alexander the Great invaded the country late in 332 BC, he had no difficulty in quickly terminating the hated rule of Persia. Although it is impossible to deny the vigour and skill with which the Egyptians met these challenges, our survey might easily create the impression of a nation subjected for generations to considerable discontinuity. When we turn to cultural phenomena, a very different picture emerges.

The visual arts are paradigmatic. They demonstrate a determination to draw upon the traditions of the Old, Middle, and New Kingdoms, as well as the Kushite Period. Local loyalty is, if anything, even stronger than of old: Udjahorresnet insisted at the beginning of the 27th Dynasty on the sterling service he had done for his native city. A fourth-century inscription in the temple of Harsaphes in his hometown of Herakleopolis Magna indicates that such service was transmuted into devotion to the local god, a natural formulation common at this time. The devotion to local gods is easily paralleled earlier, but its prominence in the Late Period is very marked, originating, no doubt, in the political fragmentation that was endemic after the collapse of the New Kingdom.

Living in harmony with Maat is described in the tomb of Petosiris as 'The Way of Life'. A frequently mentioned stimulus to follow this path is divine influence operating on the heart of the individual—the source of his moral being. Despite this strong conviction that justice would ultimately be done, there was a carpe diem philosophy expressed, revealing that the Egyptians had lost little of their love for life. It is not surprising to find the appearance of occasional protests against the unfairness of an early death, which has prevented people from enjoying all that life has to offer. We are not confronted with a complete novelty. Demography The demography of Roman Egypt during the first three centuries AD is well-documented, thanks to approximately 300 papyri recording census returns. Estimates of the population of Roman Egypt are difficult due to contradictions between the two principal historical sources.

Diodorus Siculus estimated the population in the first century BC at 3 million, whereas Josephus, writing in the first century AD, gave a figure of 7 million. 5 million, excluding Alexandria. Alexandria, one of the most populous cities in the ancient Mediterranean, is said by Diodorus to have had a population of approximately 300,000, which is not far removed from modern estimates of around 500,000. It appears that approximately one-third of households consisted of conjugal families with their siblings, or multiple families connected by kinship. The majority of the remaining households were inhabited by single individuals or families expanded by the presence of co-residing relatives.
